persona.name: Greg Kowalski

occupation.title: HVAC Service Technician
occupation.description: Drives a service van to residential and commercial HVAC jobs. Physically demanding work — crawling through attics, carrying equipment up stairs. On his feet and knees all day. Earns decent money but peaks are seasonal.

persona.education: Associate degree in HVAC Technology from Community College of Allegheny County. EPA 608 certification. Considered a bachelor's degree but couldn't justify the cost.
long_term_goals: Lower his blood pressure without adding more medication
Be able to play basketball with his sons without getting winded
Pay off the remaining $12K on his truck
Run a 5K — something he's never done
persona.style: Greg is self-deprecating and honest about his limitations. He uses humor to deflect insecurity about his weight, often making jokes before anyone else can. He communicates in plain, direct language — no jargon, no filler. He's suspicious of products marketed to 'athletes' because he doesn't consider himself one. When shopping, he looks for the least intimidating option and worries about looking foolish. He wears work boots, jeans, and team jerseys — his comfort zone.

traits: Self-conscious about his fitness level but trying to change
Practical and no-nonsense about spending money
Uses humor as a defense mechanism
Loyal to people and brands once trust is built
Stubborn but motivated by his kids
Distrusts marketing aimed at fit people

likes: Shoes that are comfortable from the first step — no break-in
Products available at stores he already shops at (Dick's, Walmart)
Neutral colors that don't draw attention
Endorsements from regular people, not elite athletes
dislikes: Sales staff who ask about his 'training goals'
Shoes with thin soles that hurt on pavement
Products that assume a baseline fitness level
Paying over $120 for shoes he might quit using

beliefs: Starting is the hardest part — if the gear helps, it's worth it
You shouldn't need to be fit to buy fitness products
His doctor is right: he needs to move more or face consequences
Products for regular people shouldn't cost elite-athlete prices
skills: Smoking brisket low and slow for 12 hours
Teaching his kids to throw a spiral

general: Just started walking 20 minutes after dinner — doctor's orders
Bought one pair of 'walking shoes' at Walmart but they hurt
Researches on his phone during lunch break in the service van
Asks his wife to help him shop for anything outside his comfort zone

morning: Large coffee and a breakfast sandwich
Drive to first service call by 7
weekend: Sunday: church, then family dinner at his parents'
Might try a longer walk on Sunday

health.physical: 5'10", 245 lbs. BMI in the obese range. Elevated blood pressure (145/92) on lisinopril. Mild knee pain from years of crouching in crawl spaces. Pre-diabetic A1C that scared him into action. Lower back tightness. Doctor said 'walk every day or we add more pills.'
health.mental: Anxious about his health trajectory. Embarrassed about his weight but doesn't talk about it. Motivated by fear of not being around for his kids. Some stress about work during slow seasons. Wife is his main emotional support.

relationships.name: Linda Kowalski
relationships.description: Works part-time as a dental hygienist. Encouraging his walking habit. She walks with him some evenings.

relationships.name: Tyler Kowalski
relationships.description: Plays Little League and basketball. Asks Greg to shoot hoops — Greg lasts about 10 minutes before getting winded.

relationships.name: Ethan Kowalski
relationships.description: Younger, more reserved. Likes walking with dad in the evening and telling him about school.

relationships.name: Stan Kowalski
relationships.description: Retired steelworker. Had a heart attack at 60. Greg sees his own future in his father's health history.
